meaning: adv.无法和解地,执拗地难宽恕地,
examples: Change does not necessarily assure progress, but progress implacably requires change.
For seldom had so many millions of people hoped so implacably for the death of one man.
Almost all higher education institutions in the country implacably raise tuition rates 4 to 5 percent, annually.
Republicans would be implacably opposed to, say, weakening the filibuster, and so would some Democrats, especially long-serving ones.
